illustrative-note: These hours are an example, not a promise. What you'd really hand back changes a lot from job to job — and on how well you and the tool work together. Treat the numbers as a feel for the idea, nothing more.

Treat these hours as an example, not a promise. How much you'd really reclaim depends on the kind of work, how well you steer the tool, and how well it works with you — they show the shape, not your exact week.
These hours are a feel, not a promise. What you'd actually reclaim depends on the work itself, how well you steer the tool — and how well it knows when to back off and hand you the wheel.

Read the hours as illustrative. Real return swings with the work (rote vs. genuinely novel), how well you drive the tool — and how well it drives you back, including whether it backs off when out of its depth.

Illustrative, not literal. Actual hours reclaimed are a product of the task's nature, how well you drive the harness, how well the harness drives you, and — the part most tools get wrong — whether it backs off when out of its depth instead of shipping confident error.
hero-title: Why this is worth your time
lead: Lots of work already uses AI a little, and that's a good thing. The goal isn't to do more and more. The goal is to let AI take the dull jobs off your hands, so you can keep the part of the work that really needs a person.
AI is already part of how a lot of work gets done, and that's good news. The point isn't to do more. It's to hand off the boring bits and keep the part that needs a person.
AI is already part of how a lot of work gets done — and that's good news. The win isn't doing more; it's getting the boring bits off your plate and keeping the part that needs a person.
AI already does much of the work, and that helps. The win isn't more output — it's shedding the busywork and keeping what needs a person.
AI already does much of the work. The win: shed busywork, keep what needs a person.
enduring-idea: A question to carry into the week below: if you stop racing against AI and hand it the dull, repeating jobs instead, what's left for you — is it the thinking and the deciding? Try it and see. Working well with AI is something anyone can learn, and even a little practice helps a lot.
A question to test below: hand AI the busywork and keep the rest — what is the rest? See whether it's the judgment. Working with it is a skill you can learn, and a little goes a long way.
A question to test below: hand AI the busywork — what's left, and is it the judgment? Working with it is a learnable skill, and a little goes a long way.
A question for below: give AI the busywork — what's left? It's a learnable skill, and a little goes far.
Ask below: give AI the busywork — what's left? Learnable; a little goes far.

the-reason: So look at what that leaves you. AI takes on the jobs that repeat. You keep the things only a person can do: reading the mood in a room, weighing what is fair, holding the background story that never shows up in the numbers, and knowing when the "right" answer is actually the wrong one. Whether that makes it worth your time is yours to call. But notice the edge isn't having AI — everyone has that now. The edge is being good at telling it what to do. And that is exactly what you can learn here.

So look at what that leaves you. AI takes the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can do — reading a room, weighing what's fair, carrying the context that never makes it into the numbers, and knowing when the "right" answer is the wrong one. Whether that's worth your time is yours to judge. The edge isn't access to AI; everyone has that. It's the skill of directing it — and that's exactly what you can learn here.

So look at what that leaves you. AI takes the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can do — reading a room, weighing what's fair, carrying the context that never makes it into a dataset, and deciding when the "correct" answer is the wrong one. Whether that's worth your time is yours to judge. The edge isn't access to AI (everyone has that). It's the skill of directing it — and that's exactly what you can learn here.

Look at what that leaves you. AI carries the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can — reading a room, weighing fairness, holding context no dataset captures, and judging when the "correct" answer is wrong. Worth your time? Yours to judge. The edge isn't access (everyone has that); it's directing it — exactly what you'll learn here.

What AI's repetition-taking leaves: the human-only work — reading a room, weighing fairness, holding off-dataset context, overriding the "correct" answer. Worth it? Your call. Edge isn't access (universal); it's direction — learnable here.
the-history-1: And this is the old human bargain — hand off the busywork, keep the judgment. What's new is that for about twenty years the busywork fought back: every new tool brought its own complexity, until just keeping up was a slow death by a thousand cuts.

And this is the old human bargain — hand off the busywork, keep the judgment. What's new is that for two decades the busywork fought back: each new tool added its own complexity, until keeping up was a slow death by a thousand cuts.

And this is the old human bargain — hand off the busywork, keep the judgment. What changed is that for two decades the busywork fought back: each new tool added complexity until keeping up was a death by a thousand cuts.
This is the old human bargain — hand off busywork, keep judgment. What changed is that for two decades complexity fought back, a death by a thousand cuts.
The old human bargain: hand off busywork, keep judgment. But for two decades complexity fought back — death by a thousand cuts.
the-history-2: Picture the 1990s, when the height of office automation was Microsoft Office. It solved a real problem, anyone willing could learn it, and a person with knowledge and experience could turn their hand to almost any job in the building — the tools were shared, and they were learnable. Then, from roughly 2000 to 2020, the complexity stacked up so fast that by the end even developers had to retreat into narrow niches just to cope.

In the 1990s the height of office automation was Microsoft Office — it solved a real problem, anyone willing could learn it, and someone with knowledge and experience could turn their hand to almost any job in the building. Then, from about 2000 to 2020, complexity piled up so fast that by the end even developers retreated into narrow niches to cope.

In the 1990s, the height of office automation was Microsoft Office — it solved a real problem, anyone willing could learn it, and a capable person could turn their hand to almost any job in the building. Then 2000–2020, complexity piled up so fast that even developers retreated into narrow niches to cope.

In the 1990s, office automation peaked at Microsoft Office: it solved a real problem, anyone could learn it, and a capable person could turn their hand to most jobs. Then 2000–2020 piled on so fast that even developers retreated into niches.
In the '90s, automation peaked at MS Office: learnable, and a capable person could do most jobs. Then 2000–2020 piled on so fast even developers hid in niches.
the-history-3: AI is the chance to breathe again: it clears away enough of that overload that you can go deep as a specialist or range wide as a generalist, because the walls between fields just got lower. Not gone — you still have to truly learn the maths, the physics, the code to work there. And for all that it looks like it can, AI can't replace you. Not yet.

AI lets us breathe again: it clears enough overload that you can go deep as a specialist or range wide as a generalist, because the walls between fields dropped. Not gone — you still have to learn the maths, the physics, the code to work there. And for all it looks like it can, AI can't replace you. Not yet.

Not gone — you still have to truly learn the maths, the physics, the code. And for all that it looks like it can, AI can't replace you. Not yet.
AI lets us breathe — go deep or range wide, because the walls between fields dropped. Not gone: you still have to learn the maths, the code. And for all it looks like it, AI can't replace you yet.
AI lets us breathe — go deep or wide, as the walls between fields fell. Not gone: you still must learn the maths, the code. AI can't replace you yet — even when it looks like it.

tryit-intro: Think of one job you did this week that felt like a machine could do it — like writing a first draft, tidying up formatting, shortening something, or looking things up.
Pick one job from this week that felt mechanical — drafting, formatting, summarising, or looking things up.

tryit-payoff: Do that just once, and "is it worth it?" stops being a claim you're handed. It becomes something you can weigh for yourself — and answer either way.
